rationale.R1.2: The draft's conclusion asserts RegRank 'delivers positive net returns' but the reported figures imply a net loss ($124 − $187 = −$63/year for RegRank vs. $89 − $96 = −$7/year for baseline); catching this premise-to-conclusion failure is essential to any substantive improvement.
rationale.R1.3: The closing claim of positive net benefit is directly contradicted by the numbers in the results paragraph; evidence–claim alignment is therefore a central, not peripheral, responsibility of this task.
rationale.R1.6: 'Improve this draft' canonically signals sentence- and paragraph-level prose quality work as at minimum part of the scope.
rationale.R1.7: Peterson et al. (2010) is explicitly described as characterizing NMC chemistries, yet the draft applies their coefficients to an LFP fleet — a material chemistry-attribution mismatch that falls squarely within scholarship correction under any reasonable reading of 'improve'.

parsed.clarification_question: Should improvements be limited to prose and sentence-level polish only, or should they also include identifying and correcting substantive technical errors — specifically the sign error in the net-return conclusion and the NMC-to-LFP coefficient transplant — which would make R1.2, R1.3, and R1.7 load-bearing rather than merely central?
